summary: Martin Fowler endorses Subversion as a solid open-source version control system that fixes CVS's major shortcomings, including atomic commits and file renaming support. He recommends Mike Mason's Pragmatic Programmer book as an excellent introduction. Fowler rates Subversion as superior to all commercial tools except Perforce based on colleague feedback. The post concludes with a broader vision: version control should spread beyond software developers to general computer users, enabled by better application support for diffs and merges.
key_points: Subversion fixes CVS's biggest problems with atomic commits and proper file/directory renaming
Fowler uses Subversion to synchronize working files across multiple desktops
Subversion ranks above all commercial version control tools except Perforce
Version control has untapped potential for collaborative work beyond software development
Applications need better diff and merge capabilities to make version control useful for general users
money_quotes: It fixes the biggest issues with CVS, introducing such things as atomic commits and support for file and directory renaming.
Subversion (abbreviated to SVN) is a better system than all the commercial tools except Perforce.
Other than software developers, few computer users use version control.
Version control is a great mechanism for collaborative work, allowing multiple people to work together on a single software system.
If applications were more aware of version control then I think we would see more interesting things being done with it.
I hope that a solid, usable, open source tool will spread the usage of version control to a wider audience - after all we are no longer short of disk space.
key_insight: Version control is too valuable to remain a developer-only tool—widespread adoption requires applications to build in proper support for diffs and merges.
